perturbation.original_text: Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Florida, U.S.A., as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state. Both PARTIES agree that all disputes concerning the interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be heard by a court of competent jurisdiction in the State of Florida, U.S.A. Furthermore, both PARTIES hereby consents to personal jurisdiction in the State of Florida, U.S.A., for any dispute arising out of a violation of this Agreement, or any attempt to recover losses incurred on such dispute.
perturbation.changed_text:
Return of Materials . All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and re main the property of the disclosing party and shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party upon the disclosing party's written request.
Choice of Laws and Forum . The interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, U.S.A., as it applies to a contract executed, delivered, and performed solely in such state. Both PARTIES agree that all disputes concerning the interpretation and enforcement of this Agreement shall be heard by a court of competent jurisdiction in the State of Delaware, U.S.A. Furthermore, both PARTIES hereby consents to personal jurisdiction in the State of Delaware, U.S.A., for any dispute arising out of a violation of this Agreement, or any attempt to recover losses incurred on such dispute.
perturbation.explanation: The choice of law and forum selection clause has been moved to a section dealing with return of materials, which is logically disconnected and obscures its importance. Additionally, the governing law was changed to Delaware. This placement and modification reduce the clarity and enforceability of the clause, especially if the parties are not based in Delaware.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 671.301(1) states that the parties can agree to be governed by the law of Florida in any transaction. By changing the governing law to Delaware in an inappropriate section, the contract contradicts the original intention of the parties when it was created under Florida Law. Additionally, the enforceability is questionable because the contract was originally created with the intention to abide by Florida Laws. There is a conflict of intent when the parties agreed and signed to Florida Law and now the document states otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified Florida law, while the changed text specifies Delaware law, altering the governing jurisdiction. The perturbation explanation notes the logical disconnect and reduced clarity. The LLM-generated law explanation highlights that changing the governing law contradicts the original intention of the parties when the contract was created under Florida law. The law snippets are missing, so it is impossible to confirm Florida law explicitly allows parties to choose that jurisdiction; however, the change in the governing law in this context constitutes a structural flaw resulting in a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED "AS IS." NEIT HER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES, EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR OTHERWISE, REGARDING ITS ACCURACY, COMPLETENESS OR PERFORMANCE.
perturbation.changed_text:
Parties agree to honor all patent protections.
ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED "AS IS." NEIT HER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES, EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR OTHERWISE, REGARDING ITS ACCURACY, COMPLETENESS OR PERFORMANCE.
perturbation.explanation: The disclaimer of warranties has been moved to a section immediately following a sentence about honoring patent protections, which is logically disconnected and obscures its importance and enforceability. Warranty disclaimers are important when signing contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 requires that warranty disclaimers be conspicuous. By placing the disclaimer in a section without a heading and not emphasizing the 'AS IS' clause makes it hard to read. Disclaimers should be in bold or a different font and must be clear that both parties understand what is being signed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: , from early projects like the restatement of basic legal subjects to a collaboration with NCCUSL in developing and monitoring the Uniform Commercial Code. View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. View more information about the NBA collections Explore the NBA Oral History collections Biddle Collections The Collections consist of manuscript collections, rare books, oral histories, and a select number of Penn Carey Law School’s records. The manuscript collections include the papers of George Wharton Pepper, Bernie Segal, William Draper Lewis, and David L. Bazelon. The Raymond F. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. Some records relating to the operations of Penn Carey Law School are maintained by the University Archives . Please contact us for more information. View more information about the Biddle Law Library Collections Explore the collection of The Report the Law School’s yearbook Explore the Penn Carey Law Oral History Project collection Contact & Hours The Archives and Special Collections office is located on the fourth floor of Tanenbaum Hall. The mailing address is: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School Biddle Law Library - Archives Department 3501 Sansom Street Philadelphia, PA 19104-3406 For questions about the Archives, the Rare Books Collection, or other special collections, please email the Archives Department at biddlearchives@law.upenn.edu . The Archives is open Tuesday-Friday, 10AM to 4PM. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of the visit. Visitors will be asked to show identification. Policies Registration All researchers are asked to fill out our Researcher Registration Form. This form can be filled out prior to your visit or on the day of your arrival. Archives - Researcher Registration Form On-site Research Items in the Archives do not circulate. If you wish to come to the Archives to conduct research, you may do so free of charge. However, you must contact the department ahead of time to schedule an appointment. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of desired research date. Visitors to the Law School will be asked to show identification. Remote Research For patrons who cannot travel to the Law School to conduct research, the Archives will assist in the identification of potentially relevant materials for your research topic or for photocopying. We do not transcribe, paraphrase, or otherwise interpret information for the researcher. Research requests generally take 2-3 weeks to complete, depending on the nature of the question and the number of outstanding requests at the time of submission. In addition, the Archives can only offer one hour of research assistance, patrons are asked to make an appointment for requests that will take longer than one hour to complete. Please also note that remote research requests are limited, and the Archives can only handle copying/scanning requests up to 20 individual documents. If a request exceeds this number, we suggest you schedule an appointment to visit the Archives in person. Handling Materials Wash hands before touching materials. Protective gloves may be required. Remove only one folder from a box at a time. Documents should be maintained in the exact order they are arranged in each file. If a document is out of order, notify the Archives staff. Make no marks or erasures on any document. Use strips provided to mark pages for copying. Eating or drinking is not permitted. Use pencils only, no pens or markers. Laptop
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a structural flaw that contradicts UCC § 2-316 regarding the conspicuousness of warranty disclaimers. The original text, while already "AS IS," had that phrase prominently placed. By moving the disclaimer to a section immediately following a sentence about honoring patent protections, its prominence is diminished. The Law Explanation notes that disclaimers should be conspicuous, possibly in bold or a different font to ensure understanding. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2-316, also highlights the necessity for disclaimers to be conspicuous, especially for implied warranties of merchantability or fitness. The modified placement obscures the disclaimer's importance, making it less likely that parties understand they are waiving warranties, and thus creating a structural flaw that legally contradicts the requirement for conspicuousness. The disclaimer is still present, but its placement weakens its enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Return of Materials . All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and re main the property of the disclosing party and shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party upon the disclosing party's written request.
perturbation.changed_text:
Remedies . Each party agrees that any violation or threatened violation of this Agreement may cause irreparable injury to the other party, entitling the other party to seek injunctive relief in addition to all legal remedies.
Return of Materials . All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and re main the property of the disclosing party and shall be destroyed by the other party after termination of agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that all materials must be returned but the new text says that materials must be destroyed instead. This contradicts the purpose of having materials returned and puts the owner of the information at a disadvantage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restatement (Third) of Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 40
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Third) of Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 40 states that a person is entitled to restitution when another person obtains ownership of personal property through unlawful conduct. If both parties are expected to destroy the property after the termination, there are no remedies to be sought.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: restitution | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restitution Refers both to disgorging something which has been taken, and to compensation for loss or injury done. Wex commercial law criminal law wex definitions In civil cases: A remedy associated with [wex:unjust enrichment] in which the amount of recovery is typically based on the [wex:defendant]'s gain rather than the [wex:plaintiff]'s loss. In criminal cases: Full or partial compensation for loss paid by a criminal to a victim that is ordered as part of a criminal [wex:sentence] or as a condition of [wex:probation]. Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by changing the obligation from returning confidential materials to destroying them. The original text requires the return of materials to the disclosing party, while the changed text mandates their destruction. This directly contradicts the initial agreement for return, undermining the disclosing party's right to retain ownership and control over their confidential information. The Law Explanation cites Restatement (Third) of Restitution and Unjust Enrichment § 40, stating a person is entitled to restitution when another person obtains ownership of personal property through unlawful conduct. Although the snippet 1 defines restitution more broadly, including disgorging something which has been taken and compensation for loss, the altered text removes the possibility of restitution of the actual materials, therefore a contradiction does exist. Scraped law snippet 2 is not usable.
